title: Listening - Part 4: Designing Sensory Gardens
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Priya Shah, a designer of sensory gardens. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m speaking to Priya Shah, who designs sensory gardens for schools, hospitals and public spaces. Priya, what exactly is a sensory garden? || Priya: It is a garden planned so people can enjoy it through touch, scent, sound and movement as well as sight. I became interested when my aunt began losing her sight. [24]On trips to ordinary gardens, she found that almost every sign and comment focused on colours she could no longer fully appreciate[/24]. I wanted to create outdoor spaces that offered her more ways to explore. || Presenter: How do you select plants? || Priya: Colour still matters for many visitors, but it is never my starting point. [25]I look for plants with distinctive scents or safe, interesting textures, such as soft leaves or herbs that release fragrance when touched[/25]. I avoid sharp plants near paths and choose varieties that can survive curious hands. || Presenter: What did you not expect when you first started? || Priya: I thought the planting itself would be nearly everything. But people need to move confidently through the space. [26]The design of wide level paths and places to sit turned out to be just as important as choosing flowers and herbs[/26]. A wonderful scent is no help if someone cannot reach it comfortably. || Presenter: You have created several gardens in primary schools. How do children respond? || Priya: Brilliantly. Adults often hurry through, hoping to see the overall effect, whereas children slow down. [27]Children notice tiny differences in leaves, listen for insects and invent games around the sounds produced by plants[/27]. They remind me that a garden is an experience rather than a picture. || Presenter: Is there a particular design difficulty? || Priya: Keeping interest through the seasons. A garden may be lively in June but empty in November. [28]I need to combine plants and features so there is something to smell, touch or hear even in colder months[/28]. Grasses that move in winter wind and evergreen herbs can be valuable. Even a wooden feature that makes a gentle sound can add interest. || Presenter: Do clients ever misunderstand the idea? || Priya: Yes. Some assume a sensory garden is only for visitors with disabilities. [29]I explain that a space offering shade, fragrance, texture and calm is enjoyable for everybody, while also being more accessible[/29]. Inclusive design doesn’t require separating people. || Presenter: What are you hoping to do in future? || Priya: Public parks reach far more people than private gardens. [30]I want to work with local councils so sensory features become part of ordinary park design rather than a rare special project[/30]. Everybody should be able to enjoy green spaces in their own way.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What led Priya to become interested in sensory gardens?

options.A: A relative was unable to enjoy features emphasised in ordinary gardens.
options.B: She noticed school gardens lacked enough colour.
options.C: A hospital requested a garden for its patients.

questions.explanation: Her aunt's sight loss meant gardens that mainly discussed colour were less accessible to her.

questions.question: What does Priya prioritise when selecting plants?

options.B: Their strong colours throughout the year.

questions.explanation: She begins with fragrance and touch, choosing safe interesting textures.

questions.question: What surprised Priya about designing these gardens?

options.A: Paths and seating were as essential as the plants.
options.B: Many popular flowers were impossible to grow.
options.C: Visitors preferred gardens without places to sit.

questions.explanation: She learned that comfortable movement and seating matter as much as plants.

questions.question: What does Priya appreciate about children's visits?

options.A: They are keen to learn plant names.
options.B: They pay close attention to small sensory details.

questions.explanation: She says children notice differences in leaves, sounds and insects.

questions.question: What design challenge does Priya mention?

options.A: Maintaining sensory interest at different times of year.
options.B: Protecting plants from children in winter.
options.C: Finding plants which grow in public parks.

questions.explanation: She must create experiences through scent, touch or sound even in colder months.

questions.question: What misunderstanding do some clients have?

options.A: Sensory gardens need too much maintenance.
options.B: They are intended only for people with disabilities.
options.C: They cannot be combined with public parks.

questions.explanation: Priya explains that sensory gardens benefit everyone while improving access.

questions.question: What does Priya want to do next?

options.B: Open a centre for garden design courses.
options.C: Make sensory features more common in public parks.

questions.explanation: She hopes councils will include sensory elements in standard park design.
